There are these different levels of learning that we go through when we try to master anything. In other words, when you first try to do something and you know nothing about it, you're at this level, which is the lowest level called being unconsciously incompetent. Meaning you don't even know anything about what you're talking about. You wouldn't even know the first thing about the straight line. You just know that I can't close that well, but you don't know how to do it. Then all of a sudden, you hear about the straight line, you become aware of it. Like, wait a second. There's something I need to know. So now you become consciously incompetent, meaning you say, oh my god, wait, there's actually a skill set that I need to use here and perfect to close the gap here so I can do what I need to do. So you now have motivation, you have a desire to actually learn something. Once that happens, that's when learning begins. You say, you know what, there's something out there I gotta know. That was why you started studying the straight line system. You'd be a source of someone else getting results. You heard about it, saw it in the movie, whatever it would be, but that was what got you to the poison. You are consciously competent, meaning using your conscious mind, you can use the straight line system and get really good results. In other words, it's your conscious mind, not the all powerful unconscious mind that guides you through the language patterns, the tonalities, what to do first, what to do second, using your conscious forethought. Now obviously, that's not what I do. And that's not what all the people I've trained over the years do once they perfect it. They become, and I am of course, unconsciously competent, meaning that after practicing and repeating, rinsing and repeating, you get to a point where it becomes an unconscious exercise, meaning my unconscious mind now is guiding my actions. And remember that unconscious mind is all powerful.
